questions.question: Explain the difference between System V message queues and POSIX message queues with respect to their implementation and function calls.
keyPoints: System V uses msgget(), msgsnd(), msgrcv(), msgctl() while POSIX uses mq_open(), mq_send(), mq_receive(), mq_close()
System V uses numeric keys (ftok) for identification, POSIX uses file pathnames
POSIX queues appear as files in /dev/mqueue filesystem
System V supports message types for selective retrieval, POSIX uses priority levels
POSIX is simpler and standardized, System V offers more features but is more complex

questions.question: What is the Producer-Consumer problem in the context of message queues? Explain how synchronization is achieved.
keyPoints: Producer creates messages and adds to shared queue, Consumer removes and processes messages
Problem: Prevent queue overflow (when full) and underflow (when empty)
Solution uses: Mutex for mutual exclusion, Semaphores for counting (empty slots, full slots)
Producer waits if queue full (empty--), Consumer waits if queue empty (full--)
This is also known as bounded buffer problem in operating systems

questions.question: Describe the operations performed on a message queue with the help of a diagram showing producer, queue, and consumer.
keyPoints: Enqueue (or Send): Producer adds message to rear of queue
Dequeue (or Receive): Consumer removes message from front of queue
Queue acts as buffer between producer and consumer
Messages typically have: message type, priority, payload/data
Queue maintains pointers (front, rear) and count of messages
Operations can be blocking or non-blocking based on flags

questions.question: What are the advantages of using message queues in distributed systems?
keyPoints: Decoupling: Producers and consumers work independently without knowing each other
Asynchrony: Sender doesn't wait for receiver, improves responsiveness
Load Leveling: Handles traffic bursts by buffering messages
Reliability: Messages persist until processed, survive system failures
Scalability: Easy to add more producers/consumers without changing existing code
Fault Tolerance: Can implement retries, dead letter queues for failed messages

questions.question: Explain the concept of a Circular Queue. How does it differ from a Linear Queue?
keyPoints: Circular queue connects end back to beginning, forming a circle
Linear queue wastes space when front elements are dequeued (can't reuse front space)
Circular queue reuses dequeued space by wrapping front pointer to beginning
Implementation uses modular arithmetic: (rear + 1) % size to calculate next position
Solves the problem of 'queue full' even when not all slots are used in linear implementation
Both support Enqueue and Dequeue operations with O(1) time complexity

questions.question: What is a Priority Queue? How does message priority work in message queue implementations?
keyPoints: Priority queue arranges messages based on priority levels rather than pure FIFO
Higher priority messages are dequeued before lower priority ones
In System V: msgtyp parameter in msgrcv() allows selecting message by type
In POSIX: mq_send() accepts priority parameter (higher value = higher priority)
Priority can be set by sender when enqueuing the message
Useful for handling urgent/important messages that need immediate processing

questions.question: Write and explain the System V message queue functions: msgget(), msgsnd(), msgrcv(), and msgctl(). Include their function prototypes and parameters.
keyPoints: msgget(): Creates/accesses queue - int msgget(key_t key, int msgflg); Returns queue ID
msgsnd(): Sends message - int msgsnd(int msqid, const void *msgp, size_t msgsz, int msgflg); msgp contains msgtyp and message data
msgrcv(): Receives message - int msgrcv(int msqid, void *msgp, size_t msgsz, long msgtyp, int msgflg); msgtyp selects message type
msgctl(): Control operations - int msgctl(int msqid, int cmd, struct msqid_ds *buf); cmd can be IPC_STAT, IPC_SET, IPC_RMID
Example struct: struct msgbuf { long mtype; char mtext[100]; };
IPC_CREAT flag creates queue, IPC_EXCL prevents creation if exists

questions.question: Explain the concept of message persistence and reliability in message queues. Discuss various delivery semantics (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once).
keyPoints: Message persistence: How long messages survive system failures
Transient messages: Lost on broker/system crash - faster performance
Persistent messages: Written to disk - survive crashes, slower but reliable
Delivery semantics:
At-most-once: Message may be lost but never duplicated - suitable for non-critical data
At-least-once: Message never lost but may be duplicated - requires idempotent processing
Exactly-once: Message processed exactly once - requires duplicate detection and acknowledgment
Implementation requires: acknowledgments, transaction logs, message IDs, consumer idempotency

questions.question: Write a C program demonstrating the creation and usage of a POSIX message queue with producer-consumer pattern.
keyPoints: Producer process creates queue with mq_open() using O_CREAT
Producer sends messages using mq_send() in a loop
Consumer opens same queue (by name) using mq_open()
Consumer receives messages using mq_receive()
Queue attributes set maximum messages and message size
Both processes use O_WRONLY/O_RDONLY flags
Use mq_close() to close and mq_unlink() to destroy queue
Handle errors with perror() for system call failures
mq_getattr() can retrieve queue properties
Non-blocking mode possible with O_NONBLOCK flag
